turns.content: Why does this even work this way? I just tried to make a terminal spinner using the indicatif crate, but it seems like I have to manually manage tick intervals instead of just letting the library handle everything automatically. It feels like there's no built-in timer mechanism for animations in Rust.

The confusion comes from how indicatif handles timing versus rendering. The crate provides a ProgressBar struct that implements the Progress trait, but you still need to manually call tick() on your progress bar every time an event occurs. This is because Rust's standard library doesn't have a native animation timer; instead, you must use external crates like tokio::time::sleep or std::thread::sleep to introduce delays between frames. The indicatif crate wraps these timing mechanisms but leaves the actual scheduling of ticks up to your application logic.

I don't get it. If I'm writing a CLI tool, shouldn't there be some way to just say 'show spinner' and let the system handle the timing? It feels like I'm manually managing every millisecond of animation when I could just use a higher-level abstraction. Is this really how Rust works?

You're right to expect more automation, but in Rust's ecosystem, the responsibility for timing usually stays with the application layer unless you explicitly opt into async runtimes like tokio that provide better coordination. The indicatif crate doesn't magically add a global clock; it just gives you a clean interface to render frames once your code decides when to call tick(). To get true automated behavior, you'd need to integrate async runtime features or use crates like async-std for more coordinated timing within your main function.
